title: What  American thinks about Croatia - bike touring

transcript.text: Okay, what an American thinks about Croatia. Okay, I'm going to do this even though I have another 60 kilometers, so hopefully nothing eventful happens tomorrow.
And if it does, then I won't do it again.
But I feel like doing it now anyway, so.
Croatia is pretty hilly. There were lots of hills. Yesterday I went over like three big hills. Today I went over a big hill, a 380 foot hill.
But yeah, I went over like three hills yesterday that were bigger than the one today.
And it's been raining, but raining intermittently.
Not as much as the other one, the other days, last week, which was cold.
The villages are pretty much the same as in other places in Europe, you know, lots of little villages connected.
But here I've noticed that the people have their own fields where they grow food.
There's a lot of their own food going on.
So they live off the land more here than in those other countries, you know, like Czech and Slovakia and southern Poland.
They were doing it like that, though.
But here are the buildings.
They don't really finish the buildings.
They don't paint the buildings.
It's like cheap.
It's like poorer, I guess, what it looks like.
It's like, you know, just bricks and then no painting on top of it.
And then lots of bombed out villages.
I went through a section of a country yesterday that was minefields and empty buildings everywhere.
It was really weird with like graves and stuff.
So there was like a war zone there.
And it was a lot within Croatia.
But anyways, when I first came into Croatia, I noticed there are lots of people who have really dark skin, like just as dark as Mexicans, dark Mexicans.
Only, you know, their facial features are kind of European.
So I don't know.
Maybe they're the gypsies, the Roma.
And I did see some of those people like that today.
And then there's the other Croatians.
One weird thing about Croatians is I think that a lot of them wear the, like the soccer outfit.
It's blue with the white stripes.
And it's like the outfit, you know, the bottoms and the top.
Like sweat, not sweatpants, but like the soccer outfit, I guess.
I guess, yes, I kind of sweatpants, but it's funny.
Because like I saw a guy like today in this town in a street dressed like that.
I think the same exact one.
And then I saw, I was watching the video, music video, and the guy singing the video was dressed like that.
And then I remember that movie with Gene Hackman and Owen Wilson called something or whatever, where Owen Wilson gets shot down in Serbia.
And then he spends the whole, like, movie, like trying to get rescued by the helicopters.
And there's that, like, Serbian tracker after him who's dressed in the soccer outfit, man.
So, anyways, Serbians speak English.
Not as good, not very good English.
But maybe even better than the Hungarians and the Poles.
I would say better than the Poles, but not really.
But, I mean, today I was like, where's the next town?
To these younger guys in the convenience store.
And one of them could speak English.
But they pretty much don't speak English.
One weird thing that I noticed about Croatians is that there's lots of old women, like, outside doing the laboring.
Like, the cutting of the trees and the planting of the bushes and the yard work and stuff.
And, you know, usually it's men who do that, but here it's women.
And then it's, and not just Croatia, but Eastern Europe.
A lot of the people doing the manual labor outside, they're old people.
Like in America, it's Mexicans.
And then today there was this guy on a, there's lots of people on really old tractors.
Like old tractors that don't go that fast going down the road.
I saw some of those in Hungary, too.
But the ones today, here in Croatia, are even slower and older.
And people, kind of what I've noticed here, aren't, aren't, is, you know,
And it's definitely kind of been progressing, simply going down, I guess.
People aren't as, like, happy and cheery.
Like, hey, you know, like they are in, like, Holland and Germany, you know.
And then it's a little bit lesser in the western part of Austria.
But, like, Eastern Austria, they're happy.
Hey, hey, hey.
But then it was a little bit less in Czech Republic.
And then, like, but, like, but, like, here in Croatia, people, you'll look at them and they're kind of like, you know, kind of like a, I don't know, different countenance, if that's the word.
Um, but, um, um, but I don't know.
I went into a Zagreb, and, um, yeah, like, the guy that worked at the hostel was kind of like that.
But, like, I went into Zagreb, and I've noticed that there's lots of men with really long hair in Zagreb, like, like, and a lot of men, like, with the hair like me.
Like, really long hair to hair just like me.
Like, I could probably be mistaken for a Zagrebian just because of my hair.
Um, and, um, and in the center, in the central square, they have this thing where they had all these, all these people doing, like, make, building things their traditional way.
Weaving baskets, um, carving, um, you know, stuff in, uh, um, crystal glasses, uh, um, hacking away, like, carving, um, like, uh, figures and stuff.
Stuff out of, like, animals out of wood.
Um, I mean, you name it.
They had, like, everything there.
Um, guy making candles, you know.
And then they had the band there was cool, because I got to listen to some, like, traditional music.
But, yeah, and they're dressed in, like, knights outfits and, like, dressed, like, from, like, hundreds of years ago.
That's, like, you know, this, I think I read, like, in the Wikipedia article that Vienna is, like, where they mix the old and the new.
But not really, not so much Vienna as freaking Zagreb.
It was, like, a freaking museum in there.
Um, and, but lots of, like, um, um, cobblestone streets in the middle of the town in Zagreb, too.
Uh, not just Poland.
Um, yeah, and then when I went into the first day in Croatia, it was kind of weird.
Um, the guy who stamped, well, they insisted on stamping me out of Slovenia.
And I didn't get stamped in Slovenia.
There were no cops there.
And then the cops, like, wanted to slam me out of Slovenia.
And then when I went into, um, he was kind of gruff about it.
And then I went into, like, uh, Croatia a little bit after that.
Um, he wanted to stamp me in Croatia.
So I stamp him in.
And he's, like, you have money?
Oh, no, at first he's, like, where are you going?
And I'm, like, Zagreb and then Serbia.
I'm, like, why do you care anyway?
And actually somebody in the, in the, in the hostel asked me that.
Like, where did you come into, into Croatia?
Like, they're, like, spies or something.
I don't know what their deal is.
But, and then he's, like, you have money?
And I'm, like, yeah, let me see it.
And I was, like, all right, well, here's my cash.
He's, like, you have card?
And I'm, like, yeah, let me see it.
So I was, like, okay.
It's, like, what?
I'm an American.
Do you think I'm going to come to Croatia to do illegal work?
Look at me.
I've got a bike with bike clothes.
And look at my passport in all the countries I'm in.
I'm obviously bike touring.
But, anyways, he let me in.
Um, and then, yeah.
And then a little bit later I got, like,
I, I, see, I have a really big map that doesn't show all the little towns.
And so it kind of makes it difficult to, like, find which road, you know, you go because it doesn't say.
Um, and I kind of got, like, funneled into the highway.
And as I got on, it was a toll highway.
And as I got on the highway, it was a toll.
I asked the girl, I go, hey, bicycle, bicycle good highway, yes?
And she's, like, oh, yeah, just go that way.
I was, like, cool, man, bike on the highway.
Plus, I saw the sign, and it has, like, the circle around something.
It has bike, and then it has tractor, and then it has, like, horse, a guy on a horse.
But there's no X through it.
There's no cross through it.
And, you know, in America, there's a cross through it, and that says no.
But what I didn't notice is below, it says autopista with the cross through the word.
And then they have those things in front.
And so I should have been, like, duh, no bike on the highway.
And there's no bike on the highway anywhere in the world.
And I saw that same sign in Poland where I rode on the highway for, like, 10 miles.
Luckily, nobody pulled me over.
But, yeah, I'm riding down the highway, like, yay, man.
But anyway, these road worker guys pull me over, and they're, like, dude, put your bike in the car, man.
You're going to, we're going to call the cops.
You're going to have a fine.
You're going to have to pay.
And I was, like, oh, so they drive me down the road a little bit and pull me off.
And I took my bike out.
And they, like, six guys, like, from two different trucks, they, like, stand around me.
And they're, like, you have to wait here 10 minutes for cops to come and some of the cops come.
And I was explaining to those guys, I was, like, look, the sign, there's no X through the sign, number one.
Number two, the girl told me that I could go on the highway.
And I had to, like, say this with, like, sign language because none of them could speak my language of English.
And I couldn't, I can't speak Croatian.
So then the cops come, and they're, like, I'm, like, I say the same thing.
I was, like, totally trying to defend myself.
I didn't want to get a ticket.
And then they were, like, follow me.
So I ride my bike after them for, like, a couple, like, a kilometer or two.
And then they're, like, yeah, go that way.
And they're really friendly.
And they just point me on the right direction down as I grab.
And I tried to make it clear to them that little missy girl, Mrs. Girl told me that I could go on it.
So, anyways, that was nice of them not to give me my ticket and make me pay.
But, yeah, so then I rode down.
And, yeah, it was, like, lots of rolling hills and stuff.
One thing that Croatians do, though, is they honk.
Some of them, more than other countries, they honk at you when they pass you or before they pass you.
Which I guess they think that's being nice, but it's kind of annoying for me.
And then, also, they like to yell at you as they pass you, like, scare you, like, boo!
That happened only twice, but that's two times more than my entire trip.
And, you know, by kids.
But, anyways, yeah, Zagreb, Croatians are nice.
I was at the hotel here, and the guy was, I was like, oh, man, you're kind of expensive.
Where's the hotel?
Is there a hotel on the board?
And the guy who works here, he's like, oh, go to this other hotel.
He gave me directions to go there.
I said it was cheaper, so I go there, but it wasn't cheaper.
And then they had full rooms anyway, so I came back.
Okay, so I'm out of time.
That's what an American thinks about Croatia.
Okay, bye-bye.
